projects.title: Unit 10 Project — Volume & Surface Area in Action
projects.desc: Apply volume and surface area to a real-world capstone challenge for Unit 10.

projects.title: Volume Vault
projects.desc: Crack the vault by computing volumes of rectangular prisms (6.G.2).

projects.title: 3D Volume Builder
projects.desc: Build prisms in 3D and find their volume by packing unit cubes (6.G.2).

projects.title: Surface Area Packer
projects.desc: Unfold prisms into nets and pack the surface to find total area (6.G.4).

title: Volume with Whole Number Edges
contentObjective: I can find the volume of a rectangular prism with whole-number edges using length × width × height.
languageObjective: I can explain my work using the words volume, rectangular prism, cubic units, and edge.

turnAndTalk.question: Your time capsule container is a rectangular prism. To find how much it can hold, why do you need three measurements (length, width, and height) instead of just one or two?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Volume measures space in three directions, so you need length, width, and height.

stems.en: I need three measurements because volume fills space in ___ directions.
stems.es: Necesito tres medidas porque el volumen llena el espacio en ___ direcciones.

stems.en: Length and width alone only give the ___, not the volume.
stems.es: El largo y el ancho solos solo dan el ___, no el volumen.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain that volume is three-dimensional, so all three edges (length, width, height) are needed; two measurements only give area of one face.
turnAndTalk.extend: Could two time capsules have the same volume but different shapes? Justify with an example.
extendStems: Two capsules ___ have the same volume but different shapes because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Container C is 8 × 3 × 4 and Container B is 5 × 5 × 2. How did you count or multiply the unit cubes to find each volume, and which holds more?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Multiply length × width × height to count all the unit cubes that fill the box.

stems.en: I found the volume by multiplying ___ × ___ × ___, which equals ___ cubic units.
stems.es: Hallé el volumen multiplicando ___ × ___ × ___, que es ___ unidades cúbicas.

stems.en: Container ___ holds more because its volume is ___.
stems.es: El contenedor ___ contiene más porque su volumen es ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students compute Container C = 8 × 3 × 4 = 96 in³ and Container B = 5 × 5 × 2 = 50 in³, and conclude C holds more by connecting volume to multiplying all three edges.
turnAndTalk.extend: Container B (50 in³) has a larger length and width than Container D (10 × 2 × 3 = 60 in³), yet D holds more. Explain how that is possible.
extendStems: D holds more even though ___ because volume depends on ___.
A bigger length or width does not always mean a bigger volume because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: A moving company's large box is 24 × 18 × 18 in and its medium box is 18 × 14 × 12 in. Talk through how you would find how many more cubic inches the large box holds.
turnAndTalk.kernel: Find each volume with V = l × w × h, then subtract the smaller from the larger.

stems.en: The large box volume is ___ in³ because 24 × 18 × 18 = ___.
stems.es: El volumen de la caja grande es ___ in³ porque 24 × 18 × 18 = ___.

stems.en: The large box holds ___ more cubic inches because ___ − ___ = ___.
stems.es: La caja grande contiene ___ pulgadas cúbicas más porque ___ − ___ = ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students compute large = 7776 in³, medium = 3024 in³, then subtract to get 4752 in³ more, and reason that you must find each volume before comparing.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why can't you just compare one dimension (like length) to decide which box holds more? Use the two boxes to justify.
extendStems: Comparing only length is not enough because ___.
Even though the medium box is shorter in one edge, ___.

turnAndTalk.question: A classmate found the volume of a box and wrote the answer as 220 in². How would you correct the units, and how do you explain why volume uses cubic units?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Volume measures three-dimensional space, so the units must be cubic (in³), not square (in²).

stems.en: The units should be ___ instead of in² because volume measures ___.
stems.es: Las unidades deben ser ___ en lugar de in² porque el volumen mide ___.

stems.en: Square units (in²) are used for ___, but cubic units (in³) are used for ___.
stems.es: Las unidades cuadradas (in²) se usan para ___, pero las unidades cúbicas (in³) se usan para ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students change in² to in³ and explain that volume fills 3D space (three edges multiplied), while square units describe flat area of one face.
turnAndTalk.extend: If you multiplied inches × inches × inches, why does that produce cubic inches? Generalize the rule for any volume unit.
extendStems: Multiplying three lengths gives cubic units because ___.
In general, the unit for volume is always ___ because ___.

vocabulary.definition: How much space is inside a solid shape.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Cuánto espacio hay dentro de una figura sólida.
vocabulary.visual: A box 3 × 2 × 4 holds 24 unit cubes, so V = 24 cubic units
vocabulary.cloze: The amount of space inside a three-dimensional solid is its ___.

vocabulary.definition: A solid box shape with six flat rectangle sides.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una figura sólida en forma de caja con seis lados rectangulares.
vocabulary.visual: A cereal box or shoe box — it has length, width, and height
vocabulary.cloze: A solid with six rectangular faces, like a box, is a ___.

examples.why: A ball is a sphere, not a prism.

vocabulary.definition: The units used to measure space inside, like cubic inches.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Las unidades para medir el espacio interior, como pulgadas cúbicas.
vocabulary.visual: A tiny cube that is 1 in × 1 in × 1 in = 1 in³ (one cubic inch)
vocabulary.cloze: The unit used to measure volume, like cubic centimeters, is ___.

vocabulary.definition: How long, how wide, and how tall a box is.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Qué tan largo, ancho y alto es una caja.
vocabulary.visual: V = l × w × h: for a box 5 × 3 × 2, volume = 30 cubic units
vocabulary.cloze: The three edge measurements of a rectangular prism are its ___.

vocabulary.definition: A flat shape that folds up into a solid.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una figura plana que se dobla y forma un sólido.
vocabulary.visual: A cross-shaped pattern of 6 rectangles folds into a rectangular box
vocabulary.cloze: A flat pattern that folds up into a solid is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: The line where two flat sides of a solid meet.
vocabulary.definitionEs: La línea donde se unen dos lados planos de un sólido.
vocabulary.visual: A cube has 12 edges — 4 along the top, 4 along the bottom, 4 vertical
vocabulary.cloze: A line segment where two faces of a solid meet is an ___.

launch.narrative: Your team is building time capsule containers to store class memories. Each container is a rectangular prism, and you need to figure out how much each one can hold. That means calculating the volume using whole number dimensions!
launch.contextImage: Students examining different rectangular box containers for a time capsule project
noticePrompts: What three measurements do you need to find the volume of a box?
What does volume tell us about a container?
How is volume different from the area of one face?
wonderPrompts: If two containers have the same volume, must they have the same shape?
What happens to the volume if you double just one dimension?

conceptIntro.heading: How do we find the volume of a box?
conceptIntro.intro: Volume is how much space is inside a 3D shape. For a rectangular prism (a box), volume = length × width × height, measured in cubic units.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: To find the volume of a box, multiply all three edges: V = length × width × height.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: My time capsule box is 6 long, 4 wide, and 3 tall. I write the formula: V = length × width × height.
I put in the numbers: V = 6 × 4 × 3.
First I multiply two of them: 6 × 4 = 24.
Then I multiply by the last one: 24 × 3 = 72.
So the volume is 72 cubic units. I use cubic units because volume fills space in 3 directions.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Now a smaller box: 5 long, 2 wide, 2 tall. What formula do we use?
Let's put in the numbers: V = 5 × 2 × 2. What is 5 × 2?
We got 10. Now multiply 10 × 2. What is the volume?
Yes, V = 20 cubic units. Don't forget the cubic units!

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: In the table below, find the volume of each container using V = length × width × height.
Write your answer in cubic inches (in³).

explore.instructions: Calculate the volume of each time capsule container. Use V = l × w × h.

discourse.prompt: Container C holds the most (96 in³) and Container B holds the least (50 in³). What do you notice about the dimensions that make one volume larger than another?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Container C has the largest volume because ___ × ___ × ___ = ___. Compared to Container B, the dimensions ___ produce a ___ volume because ___.

optional.stem: A time capsule box is 5 units long, 3 units wide, and 2 units tall. How many unit cubes fill it?
optional.explanation: V = length × width × height = 5 × 3 × 2 = 30 cubic units.

optional.stem: You stack unit cubes in a capsule: each layer has 6 cubes, and there are 4 layers. What is the volume?
optional.explanation: Each layer has 6 cubes and there are 4 layers, so 6 × 4 = 24 cubic units. This is the same as length × width × height with one factor being the number of layers.

optional.label: Match each capsule's dimensions to its volume. Use V = length × width × height.

optional.instructions: A capsule is filled with unit cubes. Sort each statement by whether it describes COUNTING cubes by layers or finding volume with the FORMULA.

items.text: Add up the cubes in every layer
Stack layers until the box is full, then count
Multiply the three edge lengths together

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Cube Stack Mistake

workedExample.label: Dimensions
workedExample.work: A capsule is 5 long, 3 wide, and 4 tall

optional.correctWork: The student added the edges instead of multiplying. Volume = 5 × 3 × 4 = 60 cubic units. Adding the edges (5 + 3 + 4 = 12) does not count the cubes that fill the box.
hints: Look at the Calculate step. Should the three edges be added or multiplied?
Volume multiplies the edges: 5 × 3 × 4 = 60 cubic units, not 5 + 3 + 4 = 12.

approaching.stem: What is the volume of a rectangular prism with l = 7 in, w = 3 in, h = 4 in?
approaching.explanation: V = l × w × h = 7 × 3 × 4 = 84 cubic inches.

approaching.stem: A time capsule box has a volume of 120 cm³. Its length is 10 cm and width is 4 cm. What is its height?
Which unit is used for volume?
approaching.explanation: Volume measures 3D space, so it uses cubic units like in³. Square units (in²) are for area, and inches are for length.

approaching.instructions: Find the volume of each rectangular prism.

approaching.label: Put these steps in order for finding the volume of a box that is 8 in long, 5 in wide, and 3 in tall.
items: Write the formula: V = l × w × h
correctOrder: Write the formula: V = l × w × h

onLevel.instructions: Complete the table. Find the missing dimension or volume for each time capsule.

onLevel.stem: Two storage bins: Bin A is 8 × 6 × 5 inches. Bin B is 9 × 4 × 7 inches. Which holds more and by how much?
onLevel.explanation: Bin A: 8 × 6 × 5 = 240 in³. Bin B: 9 × 4 × 7 = 252 in³. Bin B holds 12 in³ more.

onLevel.label: A container is 10 × 4 × 3 inches. It is divided into two equal sections. What is the volume of each section?
onLevel.questionText: Each section holds how many cubic inches?

onLevel.label: Sort these rectangular prisms from LEAST volume to GREATEST volume.

extending.title: Find the Volume Error

workedExample.label: Dimensions
workedExample.work: l = 9 ft, w = 4 ft, h = 6 ft

extending.correctWork: 4 × 6 = 24, not 10. The student added instead of multiplied. Correct: V = 9 × 24 = 216 ft³.
hints: 4 × 6 = 24, not 10. The student added 4 + 6 instead of multiplying.

extending.prompt: A box needs to hold exactly 60 cubic inches. Give three different sets of whole-number dimensions that work. Which set would make the box closest to a cube shape? Why might that matter?
extending.sentenceFrame: Option 1: ___ × ___ × ___. Option 2: ___ × ___ × ___. Option 3: ___ × ___ × ___. The ___ option is closest to a cube because ___. This matters because ___.

extending.instructions: If you double ONE dimension of a 3 × 4 × 5 box, how does the volume change?

optionalActivity.name: Cube Stack Volume Build
optionalActivity.intro: Pack each time capsule by stacking unit cubes layer by layer to find its volume.

connect.scenario: A moving company uses rectangular boxes. A large box is 24 inches long, 18 inches wide, and 18 inches tall. A medium box is 18 inches long, 14 inches wide, and 12 inches tall.
connect.promptQuestion: How many more cubic inches does the large box hold than the medium box?
connect.prompt: The large box volume is ___ in³ because V = ___ × ___ × ___. The medium box volume is ___ in³. The large box holds ___ more cubic inches.

exitTicket.stem: A rectangular prism has l = 11 in, w = 5 in, h = 4 in. What is the volume?
exitTicket.explanation: V = 11 × 5 × 4 = 220 cubic inches. Remember: volume uses cubic units (in³).
